cases.title: Tenant Lockout After Repair Dispute
strongest_arguments: California strongly disfavors landlord self-help eviction; changing the locks without judicial process is unlawful if the tenant still had a possessory interest, and the record shows no surrender, no key return, and no unlawful detainer process.

The tenant documented mold and heat outages in writing with photos, and the landlord replied that repairs would wait until next month; that supports notice, delay, and a credible habitability-based rent withholding defense under Green, Knight, and Stoiber.

The landlord’s own inspection note found furniture, medication, and work equipment still inside when the locks were changed, which powerfully undermines abandonment and is inconsistent with a completed move-out or relinquishment of possession.
vulnerabilities: The record does not quantify the severity and duration of the mold and heat defects, so the defense still has to prove they were material enough to justify withholding rent.
There is no direct evidence that the tenant tendered partial rent, paid into escrow, or otherwise mitigated the unpaid-rent narrative, which the prosecution can use against equity and credibility.

Belongings left in the unit are strong but not dispositive proof of possession; if the landlord produces evidence of keys surrendered, vacancy, or a departure statement, abandonment becomes a live issue.
evidence_gaps: Exact severity and duration of mold and heat outages.
Whether any rent tender, escrow, or partial payment was made.
Whether keys were ever surrendered or any abandonment notice was served.
Whether an unlawful detainer action had been filed before the lock change.
The tenant’s precise occupancy timeline immediately before and after the lock change.
